walkthrough.title: Unfold the Main Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Lisboa set up. First, unfold the main game board and place it in the center of the table. This is a big board showing the destroyed city of Lisbon after the earthquake of 1755. You will see the city grid in the center, the harbor at the bottom, the noble court areas on the sides, and various tracks around the edges.

walkthrough.title: Place the Rubble Cubes
walkthrough.text: Now let's fill the city with rubble. Find the rubble cubes and place them on the designated city lots on the board as shown in the setup diagram. These cubes represent the destruction from the earthquake and must be cleared before anyone can build shops there.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Noble Court
walkthrough.text: Lisboa revolves around three historical figures. Find the noble standees or markers for the Marquis of Pombal, King Jose the First, and the Patriarch. Place each one in their designated court area on the board. These three nobles are the heart of the game, and you will be visiting them throughout play.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Influence Tracks
walkthrough.text: Next to each noble you will find an influence track. Place the shared influence markers at their starting positions on these tracks. These tracks measure how much favor each player has earned with each noble and will be important for scoring at the end of the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Decree Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the decree cards and shuffle them. Draw the number specified for your player count and place them face up near the King's court area. Decrees modify the rules or provide special scoring opportunities. Return any unused decree cards to the box.

walkthrough.title: Arrange the Ships
walkthrough.text: Place the ship tokens along the harbor track at the bottom of the board. Ships are used for transporting and selling goods. Arrange them according to the setup diagram so players know which ships are available to hire during the game.

walkthrough.title: Stack the Public Building Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find all the public building tiles and sort them into their appropriate stacks. Place these stacks near the board where everyone can see them. Public buildings are major constructions that provide area bonuses to adjacent shops and are worth significant victory points.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Shop Tiles
walkthrough.text: Place the shop tiles in a supply near the board. These tiles come in different types corresponding to different trade goods. Players will claim and place these tiles on the city grid after clearing rubble from city lots.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Goods Supply
walkthrough.text: Sort the goods cubes by type and place them in a shared supply near the board. These represent the various trade goods available in Lisbon. Players will acquire goods through commerce actions and sell them for victory points and income.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Treasury Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the treasury cards. These are the main cards in Lisboa, each showing a noble and having both a treasury value and a commerce value. Give them a thorough shuffle and place the deck face down near the board. These cards are the engine that drives every turn.

walkthrough.title: Create the Card Display
walkthrough.text: Flip a row of treasury cards face up from the top of the deck to create the display. The number of face-up cards depends on your player count. This display gives players options when drawing new cards at the end of their turn.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Royal Favor Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the royal favor cards and place them in a face-down deck near the board. These bonus cards are earned by gaining political influence and provide additional scoring opportunities or special abilities throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Boards
walkthrough.text: Give each player a personal player board. This tableau tracks your wigs, which are influence tokens, your rubble, your treasury or money, and your portfolio of commerce cards. Take a moment to look at the different sections of your player board.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Wigs
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a small number of influence markers, called wigs, in their player color. Place these on your player board for now. You will spend these wigs throughout the game to gain favor with the three nobles. Wigs are one of the most important resources in Lisboa.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Rubble
walkthrough.text: Each player also receives some starting rubble cubes to place on their player board. This rubble represents personal resources you have gathered. Rubble on your player board can be spent to reduce construction costs, so do not think of it as waste.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal each player their starting hand of treasury cards from the deck. Keep your hand hidden from other players. These cards will determine your opening options, and every card can be played in two fundamentally different ways.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Victory Point Markers
walkthrough.text: Give each player a victory point marker in their color and place them on the zero space of the VP track around the board edge. Also distribute any other player-specific markers needed for tracking position on the influence tracks.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose a starting player by any method your group prefers. The first player will go first this round, and turn order may shift during the game based on gameplay decisions. Everyone is set up and ready to rebuild Lisbon. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: The Core Concept
walkthrough.text: Lisboa is a card-driven game about rebuilding Lisbon after the devastating earthquake, tsunami, and fire of 1755. On every turn, you play exactly one card from your hand. The central tension is that every card can be played in two fundamentally different ways: as a Treasury action or as a Commerce action. Choosing how to play each card is the heart of the game.

walkthrough.title: The Two Ways to Play a Card
walkthrough.text: Let's understand your two options. Option one is a Treasury action, also called a Noble Visit. You play the card face up to the matching noble's area based on its color, and you gain that noble's favor. Option two is a Commerce action. You play the card face down to your personal portfolio area and use its commerce value for trade. Every single turn comes down to this choice.

walkthrough.title: The Three Nobles
walkthrough.text: When you play a Treasury action, you visit one of three nobles. Each noble offers different benefits. The Marquis of Pombal handles city planning. The King handles royal decrees and public works. The Patriarch handles church influence. The card's color determines which noble you visit. Let's look at each one in detail.

walkthrough.title: Visiting the Marquis
walkthrough.text: When you visit the Marquis of Pombal, you gain city planning benefits. You can clear rubble from city lots, making them available for construction. You can gain building permits that let you build shops later. The Marquis also lets you manipulate the city grid in various ways. This is your main path to getting shops built in the city.

walkthrough.title: Visiting the King
walkthrough.text: When you visit King Jose the First, you gain access to royal decrees and public works. You can acquire decree cards, which modify the rules or scoring for the rest of the game. You can also obtain public building plans, which let you construct major buildings in the city. The King also grants royal favor cards as rewards.

walkthrough.title: Visiting the Patriarch
walkthrough.text: When you visit the Patriarch, you advance your standing with the church. Moving up the church influence track provides ongoing bonuses and significant end-game scoring. You also gain access to clergy bonuses and can influence cardinals. Church influence is a powerful long-term investment.

walkthrough.title: Commerce Actions
walkthrough.text: When you play a card face down for Commerce, you use its commerce value to perform trade actions. You can acquire goods, sell goods at the market, build shops on cleared city lots, or hire ships for your trading fleet. Commerce cards placed in your portfolio also contribute to your end-game scoring.

walkthrough.title: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: After you play a card on your turn, you draw one or more replacement cards. You can draw from the face-up display or from the top of the deck. The display refreshes as cards are taken. Managing your hand is crucial because the cards you hold determine which nobles you can visit and what commerce value you have available.

walkthrough.title: Clearing Rubble
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about rebuilding the city. Before you can build a shop on a city lot, you must first clear the rubble from it. You clear rubble through the Marquis or certain commerce actions. Here is the important twist: rubble you remove from the city goes to your player board. Rubble on your player board becomes a resource you can spend later to reduce construction costs.

walkthrough.title: Building Shops
walkthrough.text: Once a city lot is cleared of rubble, you can build a shop there through commerce actions. Shops generate income and victory points. Shops built adjacent to public buildings score bonus VP, so placement matters. Completing an entire street of shops triggers additional bonus scoring.

walkthrough.title: Public Buildings
walkthrough.text: Public buildings are major constructions that you build using plans acquired from the King. They are placed in the city and affect all adjacent lots. Public buildings are worth significant victory points on their own, and they boost the value of neighboring shops. Building a public building next to your own shops is ideal, but it also benefits opponents with adjacent shops.

walkthrough.title: Wigs and Influence
walkthrough.text: Wigs are your influence tokens. You spend them to place influence with the Marquis, King, or Patriarch. Advancing on the influence tracks provides ongoing benefits during the game and scores victory points at the end. Influence is a long-term investment, and falling behind on the tracks is difficult to recover from.

walkthrough.title: The Church Track
walkthrough.text: The church influence track deserves special attention. As you advance on it through the Patriarch, you unlock clergy bonuses and special scoring. The church track can provide a steady stream of points and unique abilities. Players who invest early in church influence compound their benefits over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Decrees
walkthrough.text: Decree cards are rule-modifying cards you acquire through the King. Each decree changes how certain game rules work for you specifically. Some decrees reduce costs, others enhance scoring, and some grant new abilities. Choosing the right decrees to match your strategy can create powerful combos.

walkthrough.title: Trade and Ships
walkthrough.text: Commerce actions let you acquire goods and hire ships. Ships have cargo capacity and let you transport goods for sale. Selling goods via ships provides victory points and income. Building a larger fleet enables more profitable trade routes. The shipping game is a reliable source of both money and points.

walkthrough.title: The Portfolio
walkthrough.text: Every card you play face down for Commerce goes into your portfolio on your player board. Your portfolio grows throughout the game and scores victory points at the end. This means that even when you play a card for its commerce action, you are also building toward end-game scoring. Nothing is wasted.

walkthrough.title: Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Turn order in Lisboa can shift based on gameplay decisions. Pay attention to where you are in the turn order, because going earlier gives you first pick of the card display and available city lots. Certain actions may cause you to change your position in the turn order.

walkthrough.title: The Favor Tracks
walkthrough.text: Each noble has a favor track that provides escalating benefits as you advance. The Marquis track improves your city planning abilities. The King track grants better decrees and royal favors. The Patriarch track enhances your church scoring. Investing in at least two of the three tracks is generally necessary to remain competitive.

walkthrough.title: Street Bonuses
walkthrough.text: The city grid is organized into streets. When all the lots on a street are filled with shops, a street bonus is triggered. This provides bonus victory points to the players who contributed shops to that street. Working together to complete streets can benefit everyone, but the player who places the final shop often gets an extra reward.

walkthrough.title: Royal Favor Cards
walkthrough.text: Royal favor cards are bonus rewards earned through political influence. They provide one-time benefits or end-game scoring. You earn them primarily through the King but also through high standing on the influence tracks. These cards are a nice bonus on top of your core strategy.

walkthrough.title: Game End Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game ends when the treasury card deck is depleted and all players have had an equal number of turns. This means you can somewhat predict when the game will end by watching the deck. As the deck gets thin, start pivoting from engine building to point maximization.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring Overview
walkthrough.text: Final scoring in Lisboa has many categories. You score for shops built in the city including adjacency bonuses. You score for public buildings constructed. You score based on your position on each noble's influence track. You score from church track advancement. Your portfolio cards, decree bonuses, goods, ships, wigs, and street bonuses all contribute as well.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Shops and Adjacency
walkthrough.text: Shops score their base victory points plus bonuses for being adjacent to public buildings. A single shop next to two public buildings scores more than one sitting alone. This makes city placement one of the most strategic elements of the game. Plan where you build based on where public buildings are or will be.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Influence and Church
walkthrough.text: Your position on each of the three influence tracks converts to victory points at the end of the game. Higher positions score more. The church track similarly converts your advancement into victory points. These can be some of the highest-scoring categories if you have invested consistently.

walkthrough.title: Tiebreaker
walkthrough.text: The player with the most total victory points wins Lisboa. In the case of a tie, the player with more remaining cards in hand wins the tiebreaker. If still tied, the players share the victory.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Rebuilt Lisbon
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Lisboa! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with three example players. Each player will take different approaches so you can see how the Treasury and Commerce actions work in practice. Follow along even if you have a different player count at your table. Let's begin.

walkthrough.title: Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Each player begins with a hand of treasury cards, some wigs for influence, and some rubble on their player board. Look at your starting hand carefully. Each card has a color matching one of the three nobles and a commerce value for trade. The decision of how to use each card will define your game.

walkthrough.title: Player One Visits the Marquis
walkthrough.text: Player one looks at their hand and finds a card matching the Marquis. They play it face up to the Marquis's court area. This is a Treasury action. The Marquis grants them the ability to clear rubble from a city lot. Player one removes a rubble cube from the board and places it on their player board. That lot is now open for building.

walkthrough.title: Why Clearing Rubble Early Matters
walkthrough.text: Clearing rubble early is smart because it gives you first choice of city lots. The best lots are adjacent to where public buildings will be placed. By clearing early, player one is setting up for high-value shop placements later. Also, that rubble on their player board is now a resource they can spend.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Plays Commerce
walkthrough.text: Player two decides to play a card face down for Commerce. They place it in their portfolio area and use the commerce value to acquire goods from the supply. These goods can be sold later for victory points and income. Player two draws a replacement card from the display.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Visits the King
walkthrough.text: Player three plays a card matching the King's color as a Treasury action. By visiting King Jose, they acquire a decree card. This decree will reduce the cost of building shops for the rest of the game. Player three has just gained a permanent advantage that will compound over many turns.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Influence
walkthrough.text: Player two has been focused entirely on commerce actions for three turns now, acquiring goods and building up their portfolio. They have not visited any nobles or placed a single wig on the influence tracks. Meanwhile, players one and three have been steadily advancing their influence. Player two is falling behind on the tracks, and these positions score heavily at end of game.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Treasury and Commerce
walkthrough.text: The takeaway here is critical. You need to balance Treasury and Commerce actions throughout the game. Pure commerce builds your portfolio and trade engine, but without influence you miss ongoing benefits and major end-game scoring. Plan to alternate between Treasury and Commerce plays rather than committing entirely to one mode.

walkthrough.title: Player One Builds a Shop
walkthrough.text: Player one cleared a city lot earlier. Now they play a Commerce card to build a shop on that cleared lot. They pay the construction cost, placing a shop tile on the city grid. This shop generates income and will score victory points at game end. Player one chose a lot adjacent to a public building site for the adjacency bonus.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Building in Isolation
walkthrough.text: Player three sees an open lot on the edge of the city and builds a shop there. It is far from any public buildings and unlikely to be part of a completed street. That shop will score its base points but miss out on adjacency bonuses and street completion bonuses. The same shop placed centrally could have scored two or three times as much.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Location Matters
walkthrough.text: Always consider where you build. Shops adjacent to public buildings score bonus victory points. Shops that help complete a street trigger street bonuses. Look at the board before building and choose the lot that maximizes your scoring potential. A central shop next to a public building is almost always better than an isolated one.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Visits the Patriarch
walkthrough.text: Player two realizes they need influence and plays a Treasury card to visit the Patriarch. They advance on the church track and place a wig. The church track grants them a clergy bonus right away. This is a course correction, but they have lost several turns of compounding benefits.

walkthrough.title: Player Three Acquires a Public Building
walkthrough.text: Player three visits the King again and uses the opportunity to acquire public building plans. Next turn, they can construct this public building in the city. Public buildings are worth significant points and boost all adjacent shops. Player three is thinking about where to place it for maximum impact.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Hoarding Cards
walkthrough.text: Player two has been holding onto several high-value cards, waiting for the perfect moment to play them. But in Lisboa, every card you hold is an action you are not taking. While player two waits, the other players are advancing on tracks, clearing lots, and building shops. Tempo matters.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Play Cards, Do Not Hoard
walkthrough.text: In Lisboa, every unplayed card is a missed opportunity. You draw replacements after playing, so your hand refills. There is rarely a perfect moment, and waiting costs you turns of progress. Play cards that advance your position now, even if the play is not optimal. A good action taken today beats a perfect action taken three turns from now.

walkthrough.title: Player One Uses Rubble
walkthrough.text: Player one has accumulated rubble on their player board from clearing city lots. Now they spend some of that rubble to reduce the cost of constructing a public building. This is the dual-purpose nature of rubble in action. What seemed like debris from the earthquake is now fueling reconstruction.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Forgetting to Draw Strategically
walkthrough.text: Player three has been drawing replacement cards from the top of the deck instead of the display. They keep getting cards for nobles they do not need right now. Meanwhile, the display has cards matching the Marquis, which would let them clear the rubble they need. Always check the display before drawing blind from the deck.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use the Display
walkthrough.text: When drawing replacement cards, always scan the face-up display first. Picking a card that matches the noble you want to visit next turn or has a high commerce value for your trade plans is much better than drawing blind. The display gives you information. Use it to plan your next move.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Check
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and assess. Player one has two shops near public buildings and decent influence. Player two has a growing portfolio and trade goods but started influence late. Player three has strong decrees and public building plans but built a shop in a bad location. All three are viable because Lisboa rewards many different strategies. No one is out of the game.

walkthrough.title: Watching the Deck
walkthrough.text: As you play, keep an eye on the treasury deck. When it runs out and everyone has had equal turns, the game ends. If the deck is getting thin, start pivoting from building your engine to maximizing points. Spend your remaining wigs for influence, sell your goods, and place final shops in high-value locations.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring Preview
walkthrough.text: When the game ends, you will score across many categories. Shops plus adjacency bonuses. Public buildings. Influence track positions. Church track. Portfolio value. Decrees. Trade and ships. Street bonuses. The player who scores well across the most categories usually wins. Do not put all your eggs in one basket.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have walked through the core flow of Lisboa. You have seen how Treasury and Commerce actions work, watched three players make common mistakes and learn from them, and understood the dual-purpose nature of rubble and cards. Remember: balance your noble visits with commerce, build shops near public buildings, and do not hoard cards.

walkthrough.title: Before You Rebuild
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics and common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all the key terms, nobles, and scoring categories.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun rebuilding Lisbon!

No one is out of the game

Thin deck → pivot from engine-building to point maximization
Spend wigs, sell goods, place final shops in high-value spots

Diversified scoring across categories usually wins

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You've seen Treasury and Commerce actions in practice
Watched 3 common mistakes: ignoring influence, poor placement, hoarding cards
Key takeaways: balance noble visits with commerce, build near public buildings

summary.title: Before You Rebuild
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for terms and scoring
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we discuss strategy, here are the most common mistakes new Lisboa players make. First, playing only Treasury actions or only Commerce actions instead of balancing both. Second, ignoring the influence tracks until it is too late to catch up. Third, building shops in isolated city lots far from public buildings. Fourth, hoarding cards waiting for the perfect play instead of maintaining tempo. Fifth, forgetting that rubble on your player board is a valuable resource. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest beginner traps.

walkthrough.title: The Treasury-Commerce Balance
walkthrough.text: The most important strategic concept in Lisboa is balancing your Treasury and Commerce plays. Treasury actions advance your influence and unlock nobles' benefits, while Commerce actions build your portfolio, acquire goods, and construct shops. A rough guideline for beginners is to alternate: play a Treasury card, then a Commerce card, then Treasury again. As you gain experience, you will learn when it is right to chain two or three plays of the same type, but alternating is a safe baseline.

walkthrough.title: Invest in Influence Early
walkthrough.text: Influence works like compound interest. The earlier you place wigs on the favor tracks, the more turns you benefit from the ongoing advantages. Players who wait until mid-game to start investing in influence miss out on half the game's benefits. In your opening turns, make sure at least one of your first three plays is a Treasury action that places a wig. You do not need to go deep on all three tracks, but establishing a presence on two of them early is recommended.

walkthrough.title: Shop Placement Strategy
walkthrough.text: Where you build matters more than when you build. A shop adjacent to a public building can score double or triple what an isolated shop scores. Before building, study the board. Where are public buildings already placed? Where are public building plans likely to be constructed? Build adjacent to these areas. Also consider street completion: if a street is nearly full, placing the finishing shop triggers bonus scoring for everyone on that street, with an extra reward for the player who completes it.

walkthrough.title: Read the Card Display
walkthrough.text: Every time you draw cards, check the face-up display before pulling from the deck. The display gives you critical information. A card matching the noble you plan to visit next turn is worth its weight in gold. A card with a high commerce value can fund a major trade action. Drawing blind from the deck is only advisable when nothing in the display helps your strategy. Think of the display as a menu and choose deliberately.

walkthrough.title: Rubble Is a Resource
walkthrough.text: New players often think of rubble as an obstacle to be cleared. It is, but it is also a resource. Every rubble cube you clear from the city goes to your player board, where it can be spent to reduce construction costs later. This means clearing rubble does double duty: it opens city lots for building and stockpiles resources for cheaper construction. Plan your rubble clearing to match your building schedule.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Deck for Endgame Timing
walkthrough.text: The game ends when the treasury deck runs out and everyone has had equal turns. This means you can predict roughly when the game will end. In the early and mid-game, focus on building your engine: clearing rubble, placing wigs, acquiring decrees. As the deck thins past the halfway point, start pivoting toward scoring. Sell your goods, build your remaining shops in prime locations, and make sure your influence tracks are where you want them for final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Scoring
walkthrough.text: Lisboa has eight or more scoring categories in the final tally. The winning player almost always scores well across multiple categories rather than dominating just one. A player with fifteen points from shops, ten from influence, eight from church, five from portfolio, and five from trade beats someone with thirty from shops and zero from everything else. After every few turns, mentally check which scoring categories you are building toward and which ones you are neglecting.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't play only Treasury or only Commerce — balance both
Don't ignore influence tracks until mid-game
Don't build shops in isolated lots far from public buildings
Don't hoard cards — play them to maintain tempo
Rubble on your player board is a resource, not waste

summary.title: Diversify Your Scoring
bullets: 8+ scoring categories — diversified beats one-dimensional
Check all scoring categories every few turns
Identify which categories you're neglecting and course-correct

walkthrough.title: The City Builder Strategy
walkthrough.text: The city builder approach focuses on clearing rubble and filling the city grid with shops for maximum construction scoring. The key is to visit the Marquis early and often to clear prime lots adjacent to public building sites. Use commerce actions to build shops immediately after clearing. Accumulate rubble on your player board to reduce construction costs on later buildings. The city builder wins by dominating shop scoring and street completion bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing Shop Adjacency
walkthrough.text: The secret to the city builder strategy is adjacency optimization. Every shop adjacent to a public building scores bonus points. If you can position shops adjacent to multiple public buildings, the bonuses multiply. Study the city grid at the start of the game and identify the lots that could be adjacent to the most public buildings. Clear those lots first, even if it means spending extra actions. A single well-placed shop can be worth three or four poorly placed ones.

walkthrough.title: The Political Influence Strategy
walkthrough.text: The influence-heavy approach aims to advance on all three noble favor tracks for maximum end-game scoring and ongoing benefits. This strategy requires consistent Treasury plays visiting each noble. Place wigs early for compounding benefits. The favor tracks reward players who push far ahead, not those who spread thin. Focus on two tracks primarily and maintain token presence on the third. Influence scoring can be the largest single category if you commit to it.

walkthrough.title: Leveraging the Church Track
walkthrough.text: The church track through the Patriarch deserves its own strategic focus. Advancing on the church track unlocks clergy bonuses that provide unique abilities unavailable through other means. The church track also provides substantial end-game scoring. Players who commit early to the Patriarch often find themselves with a reliable scoring engine that other players cannot easily disrupt. Combine church advancement with decree cards that enhance religious scoring for maximum impact.

walkthrough.title: The Trade Empire Strategy
walkthrough.text: The trade empire approach builds a shipping fleet and trades goods for victory points and income. Use commerce actions to hire ships early, then acquire goods through subsequent commerce plays. Selling goods via ships provides both immediate victory points and income to fund future actions. The trade strategy is self-sustaining once your fleet is established. Look for decree cards that enhance trade profits or reduce shipping costs to supercharge this approach.

walkthrough.title: The Decree Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: Decrees are rule-modifying cards that create permanent advantages. A decree-focused strategy visits the King frequently to accumulate decrees that create powerful combos. Some decrees reduce costs, others enhance scoring, and some grant entirely new abilities. The key insight is that decrees stack. Two cost-reduction decrees together can make construction nearly free. Two scoring enhancement decrees can double your end-game points in a category. Identify decree synergies early and build your game around them.

walkthrough.title: Hybrid Strategy: Builder-Politician
walkthrough.text: The strongest Lisboa games often combine two approaches. The builder-politician hybrid uses Treasury actions to visit the Marquis for city planning while also advancing on the influence tracks. Commerce actions then fund shop construction. The synergy is that the Marquis's favor track improves your building efficiency while your shops generate income to fund wig placements. This hybrid covers two of the three highest-scoring categories.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Pivot
walkthrough.text: Every Lisboa game has a pivot point where you shift from building your engine to maximizing your score. This typically happens when the treasury deck is about one-third remaining. Before the pivot, invest in influence, clear rubble, acquire decrees, and set up your trade routes. After the pivot, cash in. Sell goods, build your remaining shops in prime locations, and spend your last wigs on the influence tracks. Players who pivot too early leave engine value on the table. Players who pivot too late run out of time to convert their setup into points.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponents
walkthrough.text: Lisboa is an interactive game. Watch what your opponents are doing and adapt. If someone is building toward a public building in a certain area, consider whether to build nearby for adjacency or elsewhere to avoid competition. If an opponent is loading up on goods to sell, the market may become less profitable. If everyone is visiting the Marquis, the King and Patriarch may offer better returns because fewer players are competing on those tracks. The best Lisboa players adjust their strategy based on what everyone else is pursuing.

walkthrough.title: Portfolio Optimization
walkthrough.text: Your portfolio of commerce cards scores victory points at end of game, and many players underestimate this category. Every time you play a Commerce action, that card enters your portfolio permanently. This means Commerce actions do double duty: they fund an immediate trade action and build toward end-game scoring. In a long game, a player who has played many Commerce actions may have ten or more cards in their portfolio, which can rival shop scoring. Factor portfolio value into your Treasury versus Commerce decision on every turn.

entries.definition: One of the two ways to play a card each turn. Play the card face up to the matching noble's court area to gain that noble's favor and associated benefits. Also called a Noble Visit.

One of the two ways to play a card each turn. Play the card face down to your portfolio area to use its commerce value for trade actions such as acquiring goods, selling goods, building shops, or hiring ships.
Another name for a Treasury action. You visit one of the three nobles — Marquis, King, or Patriarch — by playing a matching-color card face up to their court area.
Each treasury card has a color that determines which noble it can be played to for a Treasury action. The three colors correspond to the Marquis of Pombal, King Jose I, and the Patriarch.
The numerical value on a treasury card used when playing it as a Commerce action. Higher commerce values enable more powerful or costly trade actions.
A row of face-up treasury cards available for drafting when you draw replacement cards at the end of your turn. Refreshes as cards are taken. Number of cards varies by player count.
The sequence in which players take turns. Turn order can shift during the game based on certain gameplay decisions. Earlier position gives first pick of display cards and city lots.

One of the three nobles. Controls city planning. Visiting the Marquis lets you clear rubble from city lots, gain building permits, and manipulate the city grid. His favor track improves building efficiency.

One of the three nobles. Controls royal decrees and public works. Visiting the King grants decree cards, public building plans, and royal favor cards. His favor track provides better decrees and rewards.

One of the three nobles. Controls church influence. Visiting the Patriarch advances your position on the church track, grants clergy bonuses, and lets you influence cardinals. His favor track enhances religious scoring.
An influence track associated with each noble. Advancing on a favor track provides escalating ongoing benefits and end-game victory points. Wigs are placed on favor tracks to mark progress.
Influence tokens in each player's color. Spent to gain favor with the three nobles by placing them on favor tracks. Wigs are a limited resource and their placement drives long-term scoring.
Cubes placed on city lots representing earthquake destruction. Must be cleared before shops can be built on a lot. Cleared rubble transfers to the player's board as a resource.

Rubble cubes on your player board, accumulated from clearing city lots. Can be spent to reduce the cost of certain constructions, especially public buildings. Treat player rubble as a valuable resource, not waste.
Trade goods acquired through commerce actions. Come in various types. Can be sold at the market or via ships for victory points and income. Remaining goods at game end may also score.
Your money or income in the game. Used to pay for various actions and constructions. Earned through selling goods, shop income, and certain noble favors.

The main cards in Lisboa. Each card has a color matching a noble and a commerce value. Every card can be played as either a Treasury action (face up to a noble) or a Commerce action (face down to portfolio).

Rule-modifying cards acquired through the King. Each decree changes how certain game rules work for the holding player. Effects include cost reduction, scoring enhancement, and new abilities. Decrees can stack for powerful combos.
Bonus cards earned through political influence, primarily via the King. Provide one-time benefits or end-game scoring opportunities. A supplemental scoring source on top of your core strategy.
Your personal collection of face-down commerce cards on your player board. Grows each time you play a Commerce action. Scores victory points at end of game based on the number and value of cards.
The central area of the main board representing destroyed Lisbon. Divided into lots organized along streets. Lots must be cleared of rubble before shops or public buildings can be placed on them.

An individual space on the city grid where a shop or public building can be placed. Lots start covered in rubble. After clearing, the lot is available for construction. Lot position determines adjacency bonuses.

A tile built on a cleared city lot through commerce actions. Shops generate income and score victory points at game end. Shops adjacent to public buildings receive bonus VP. Different shop types correspond to different trade goods.

A major construction placed in the city using plans acquired from the King. Worth significant VP on their own. All shops adjacent to a public building receive bonus VP, regardless of which player owns them.

A row of city lots on the grid. When all lots on a street are filled with shops, a street bonus is triggered, providing extra VP to contributing players. The player placing the final shop may receive an extra reward.

Extra victory points earned by shops that are located next to public buildings on the city grid. A shop adjacent to multiple public buildings receives bonuses from each. Adjacency is one of the most impactful scoring mechanics.
Authorization gained through the Marquis that allows you to build shops on cleared city lots. Required before construction can begin on a lot.
Tokens hired through commerce actions. Each ship has cargo capacity for transporting goods. Selling goods via ships provides VP and income. A larger fleet enables more profitable trade.
The area where goods are sold for VP and income. Goods can be sold directly at the market or transported via ships for additional bonuses. Market prices may vary based on supply and demand.
The path goods take from your supply through your ships to the market. More ships and more goods enable more profitable trade routes. Trade scoring can be a significant VP source.

A special influence track advanced through the Patriarch. Provides clergy bonuses, unique abilities, and substantial end-game scoring. Early investment on the church track compounds throughout the game.
A reward unlocked by advancing on the church track. Clergy bonuses provide unique abilities not available through other game actions. Higher positions on the church track unlock stronger bonuses.
A benefit gained through the Patriarch related to influencing cardinals within the church hierarchy. Provides additional scoring and strategic options connected to the church track.
Player-colored markers placed on the noble favor tracks to show advancement. Each player has a limited supply. Advancing markers provides ongoing benefits and end-game scoring.
End-game VP earned from shops built in the city. Includes base VP per shop plus adjacency bonuses from nearby public buildings. One of the primary scoring categories.
End-game VP earned from public buildings you have constructed. Public buildings are worth significant individual VP. They also generate adjacency bonuses for nearby shops.
End-game VP based on your final position on each noble's favor track. Higher positions on the Marquis, King, and Patriarch tracks all convert to VP. Can be one of the highest-scoring categories.
End-game VP based on your advancement on the church influence track. Provides substantial points for players who invested in the Patriarch throughout the game.
End-game VP from the commerce cards accumulated face-down in your portfolio area. More cards and higher values provide more points. Grows naturally from Commerce action plays.
End-game VP from certain decree card bonuses. Not all decrees provide direct VP, but those that do can be significant. Decree scoring stacks with other bonus effects.
End-game VP from goods sold and ships owned during the game. Includes points from market sales and shipping bonuses. A reliable supplemental scoring category.
VP awarded when all lots on a city street are filled with shops. Bonus goes to all contributing players. The player who places the final shop may receive an additional reward.
The scoring track around the edge of the main board. Players advance their VP markers along this track throughout the game as they earn points. Final positions are tallied at game end.

Each player's personal tableau that tracks wigs, rubble, treasury, and portfolio. Rubble accumulates here from clearing city lots. Portfolio cards are placed face-down here. Your player board is your personal resource hub.
The central game board depicting post-earthquake Lisbon. Contains the city grid, harbor, noble court areas, influence tracks, church track, and VP track. All players interact with this shared board.
The area at the bottom of the main board where ships are docked and trade occurs. Ships are hired from the harbor and used to transport goods for sale. The harbor track shows available ships.

The game ends when the treasury card deck is depleted and all players have had an equal number of turns. Watch the deck thickness to predict when the game will end and time your scoring pivot accordingly.
If two or more players have the same VP total, the player with more remaining cards in hand wins. If still tied, the players share the victory.

The process of establishing ongoing benefits through influence tracks, decrees, and shop income that compound over time. In Lisboa, your engine comes from noble favor, decree combos, and trade infrastructure rather than a single mechanism.

The strategic shift from building your engine to maximizing point output, typically when the treasury deck is about one-third remaining. Before the pivot, invest in infrastructure. After, convert that infrastructure into VP.
